title: One-Step and Few-Step Diffusion Models
concept: Advanced distillation techniques can compress diffusion sampling down to just one or a handful of steps, approaching GAN-like generation speed.

exercises.prompt: Normal diffusion models create an image by slowly removing noise over many steps. What's a downside of this?

options.text: It can be slow because it takes many steps
It can only make black and white images

exercises.explain: Yep! Doing 50-1000 tiny denoising steps takes time. Scientists wanted a way to skip ahead and still get great results.

cards.title: What if AI could skip to the finish line?
cards.body: Imagine an artist who needs 1,000 small pencil strokes to finish a sketch. Now imagine a magic trick that lets them draw the whole picture in ONE confident stroke, and it still looks amazing. That's exactly what one-step and few-step diffusion models do for AI image generation!

cards.title: One-Step & Few-Step Diffusion
cards.body: These are special diffusion models trained (using a technique called distillation) to jump from pure noise to a finished image in just 1 to 4 steps, instead of the usual 50-1000 steps. A 'student' model learns to mimic the entire long process of a 'teacher' model, but compressed into a tiny number of jumps.

cards.title: The Marathon Shortcut Runner
cards.body: A regular diffusion model is like running a marathon step by step, checking your route at every mile. A distilled few-step model is like a runner who has memorized exactly where the finish line is and sprints straight there in just a few giant leaps, arriving at (almost) the same place, way faster.

cards.title: Real-World Speed Boost
cards.body: Techniques like Progressive Distillation, Consistency Models, and LCM (Latent Consistency Models) let apps generate an image in a fraction of a second instead of many seconds. This is why some AI art apps can now show you a picture almost instantly as you type your prompt, feeling as snappy as a GAN.

cards.title: Myth: Fewer Steps Always Means Worse Quality
cards.body: Many assume skipping steps must ruin the image. But with smart distillation training, a well-trained few-step model can produce results nearly as good as the full slow process, because it learned to compress the 'knowledge' of many steps into fewer, smarter jumps, not just skip carelessly.

cards.title: Slow Sculptor vs Speedy Sculptor
cards.body: Compare how each approach reaches a final image.

left.text: Removes noise gradually across many (50-1000) small steps, like carefully chiseling a statue bit by bit.

right.text: Trained to jump straight to the finished statue in 1-4 big steps, using knowledge distilled from the slow process.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of distillation techniques in diffusion models?

options.text: To make images bigger in resolution
To compress the many-step denoising process into just a few steps
To add more noise to images

exercises.explain: Distillation trains a faster 'student' model to mimic the full slow diffusion process using far fewer steps.

exercises.prompt: One-step and few-step diffusion models aim to approach the speed of GAN-like generation.
exercises.explain: Correct! By cutting steps way down, these models generate images almost as fast as GANs, which traditionally produce images in a single pass.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence.
exercises.sentence: A model trained to mimic a slower diffusion process but in fewer steps is created through a technique called ____.
exercises.explain: Distillation is the training technique where a student model learns to copy the results of a slower teacher process using far fewer steps.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes why a distilled few-step model can still produce high-quality images despite using fewer steps?

options.text: It randomly guesses the final image without any training
It was specifically trained to compress the knowledge of the full multi-step process into fewer, smarter jumps
It only works on black and white images so it's simpler

exercises.explain: The key is smart training (distillation), not just skipping steps carelessly. The model learns exactly how to combine steps efficiently.

exercises.prompt: You're building an app that needs to show AI-generated images to users almost instantly, like a live preview as they type. Which type of diffusion model should you choose, and why?

options.text: A classic 1000-step diffusion model, because more steps always means better quality regardless of speed
A one-step or few-step distilled diffusion model, because it can generate images almost as fast as GANs while keeping good quality
Any model works the same since step count doesn't affect speed
No diffusion model can ever be fast, so you should avoid diffusion entirely

exercises.explain: One-step and few-step distilled models are exactly designed for this use case: near-instant generation with quality close to the full slow process, perfect for live, responsive apps.
